country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

sector_signal: 5-year trajectory: technical and regulated manufacturing roles are partially automation-resistant due to regulatory compliance requirements. However, AI-assisted quality control and process optimisation will reduce headcount in some inspection and operator roles. In countries with institutional voice (EU), workers in declining roles will be retrained through works council agreements. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam), displaced workers have no institutional recourse.

country_signal: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.

country_signal: CSDDD transposition will further strengthen supply chain labour obligations. Germany's institutional worker rights framework is among the most durable globally. The key 5-year watch: automation in manufacturing (particularly relevant to DreBo Werkzeugfabrik / Interchangeable Tools) is being managed through co-determination processes, meaning automation displacement does not translate to rights erosion in the German model — works councils actively participate in restructuring decisions. country_signal: Singapore's tripartite labour model is structurally entrenched — no government has any interest in liberalising right to strike or collective bargaining beyond NTUC's managed scope. Progressive Wage Model expansion to more sectors by 2025-27 will improve formal minimum wage coverage for lower-skilled workers, but professional/office workers at Garmin Singapore are largely unaffected. Labour rights quintile is stable.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

country_signal: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.
sector_signal: 5-year: Significant automation deployment in precision manufacturing expected (cobots, CNC automation, AI-assisted quality control). Companies under cost-squeeze (consumer resistance to price pass-through) will use automation to reduce headcount rather than improve worker conditions. In countries without institutional voice, this translates to: surviving workers on increased monitoring/performance pressure; contractors replacing permanent roles; reduced benefit provisions. In EU countries, automation is managed through co-determination with maintained or improved conditions for remaining workers. This represents one of the starkest country-divergence risk factors in the 5-year view.

sector_signal: 5-year: Distribution and logistics is undergoing the fastest automation deployment of any sector in this dataset. Full warehouse automation rollout timelines of 5-8 years mean that by 2029-30, most large DC operations will be substantially automated. Remaining workers will be in highly monitored, productivity-intensive roles with reduced rights protection (algorithmic management systems are not subject to collective bargaining in most jurisdictions). This is the key sector where even countries with strong institutional frameworks (USA High labour rights baseline already) face practical rights deterioration as the employment relationship is increasingly mediated by technology rather than human management.

sector_signal: 5-year: This is the highest-risk sector in the dataset for worker rights deterioration. Toy assembly, commodity fastener, and basic hand tool manufacturing are undergoing rapid automation adoption (Fanuc, Kuka robots in Chinese facilities; vision-guided assembly robots in Vietnamese plants). As automation reduces headcount, remaining workers face increased productivity targets, algorithmic monitoring, and a weakened bargaining position (easier to threaten replacement). In countries without institutional worker voice (China, Vietnam, Thailand, India), this creates a direct rights deterioration pathway that the institutional score may lag in capturing. Consumer resistance to toy/tool price increases means cost-squeeze is near-absolute — there is no slack for voluntary rights improvements.

country_signal: Over 5 years, Mexico's labour reform trajectory points toward a genuine improvement in SDG 8.8.2 scores as legacy protection contracts are replaced with democratically-elected unions. If the reform is maintained under the Sheinbaum administration (signs are positive — she has reaffirmed USMCA labour commitments), Mexico could shift from the High to Medium quintile for labour rights by 2028-29. Nearshoring-driven wage growth (particularly in Coahuila/Nuevo León manufacturing corridors relevant to Torreon and Toluca facilities) may also improve remuneration sub-scores. Principal risk: organised crime influence on union politics in some regions could undermine the authenticity of reform gains.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

country_signal: Over 5 years, China's institutional labour rights framework shows no improvement pathway. The ACFTU reform agenda under Xi prioritises production continuity over worker advocacy. Automation in Chinese manufacturing is accelerating at state-directed pace — but unlike Germany's co-determination model, there is no institutional framework for workers to participate in or be compensated for automation-driven restructuring. Manufacturing workers displaced by automation have limited recourse. Government social insurance for work injuries (35.9% coverage) is likely to worsen as gig/platform economy expands without commensurate coverage. EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will add compliance pressure but will not change domestic rights.

country_signal: Vietnam's 5-year trajectory for worker rights is the most uncertain in the High-risk group. EVFTA pressure for ILO Convention 87 (freedom of association) compliance creates a potential (if uncertain) reform pathway — if Vietnam ratifies C87, it would be the first genuine independent union mechanism in the country's history. However, the government has resisted this for years and the base case remains: Very High institutional score maintained, with highly variable uncertainty about any reform. Sector note: TTI Vietnam and GreatStar Vietnam Base (Tools manufacturing, Hand Tools) are in export-oriented manufacturing where cost-squeeze from global supply chain competition is intense — even if statutory minimums hold, overtime enforcement and contractor conversion are likely to accelerate.

country_signal: CSDDD transposition will further strengthen supply chain labour obligations. Germany's institutional worker rights framework is among the most durable globally. The key 5-year watch: automation in manufacturing (particularly relevant to DreBo Werkzeugfabrik / Interchangeable Tools) is being managed through co-determination processes, meaning automation displacement does not translate to rights erosion in the German model — works councils actively participate in restructuring decisions. This is the critical distinction from US/Asian manufacturing contexts.

sector_signal: 5-year: Consumer-brand cost-squeeze intensification is the primary sector risk driver. As retailer power increases and private-label competition grows, branded manufacturers (SC Johnson, GOJO, Hain Celestial, JPMS) face sustained margin pressure. The supply chain response that least affects consumer price is squeezing labour costs. In countries with strong institutional frameworks (EU, Australia, UK), this pressure is mediated through collective agreements and minimum wage floors. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam, Argentina, South Africa), it translates to overtime compliance erosion, benefit reduction, and increased contractor/temp worker substitution — all rights issues even if nominal wages hold.

Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

country_signal: CSDDD and EU Labour Platform Directive will gradually improve collective bargaining frameworks for platform/flex workers. Poland's Tusk government is more aligned with EU social policy than predecessor. Minimum wage trajectory above inflation will maintain Very Low remuneration risk. Labour rights improvement is gradual — quintile shift to Low possible by 2029.
sector_signal: 5-year: Consumer-brand cost-squeeze intensification is the primary sector risk driver. As retailer power increases and private-label competition grows, branded manufacturers (SC Johnson, GOJO, Hain Celestial, JPMS) face sustained margin pressure. The supply chain response that least affects consumer price is squeezing labour costs. In countries with strong institutional frameworks (EU, Australia, UK), this pressure is mediated through collective agreements and minimum wage floors. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam, Argentina, South Africa), it translates to overtime compliance erosion, benefit reduction, and increased contractor/temp worker substitution — all rights issues even if nominal wages hold.

country_signal: Over 5 years, South Africa's labour rights picture is deteriorating in practical terms even as statutory protections remain on paper. Key trends: (1) COSATU's declining political leverage reduces its capacity to enforce sectoral agreements; (2) mass unemployment structurally weakens workers' bargaining position, meaning the ILO score for 'effective' rights (not just statutory rights) will worsen in future surveys; (3) working poverty (14.3%) is likely to increase under continued GDP underperformance, worsening remuneration sub-scores; (4) load-shedding impact on manufacturing profitability increases cost-squeeze pressure on wages and benefits. SC Johnson Rosslyn Factory (Cleaning Preparations — skilled process mfg) operates in the formal economy with SEIFSA/NUMSA coverage, but this coverage is weakening.

country_signal: Over 5 years, China's institutional labour rights framework shows no improvement pathway. The ACFTU reform agenda under Xi prioritises production continuity over worker advocacy. Automation in Chinese manufacturing is accelerating at state-directed pace — but unlike Germany's co-determination model, there is no institutional framework for workers to participate in or be compensated for automation-driven restructuring. Manufacturing workers displaced by automation have limited recourse. Government social insurance for work injuries (35.9% coverage) is likely to worsen as gig/platform economy expands without commensurate coverage. EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will add compliance pressure but will not change domestic rights.

country_signal: Vietnam's 5-year trajectory for worker rights is the most uncertain in the High-risk group. EVFTA pressure for ILO Convention 87 (freedom of association) compliance creates a potential (if uncertain) reform pathway — if Vietnam ratifies C87, it would be the first genuine independent union mechanism in the country's history. However, the government has resisted this for years and the base case remains: Very High institutional score maintained, with highly variable uncertainty about any reform. Sector note: TTI Vietnam and GreatStar Vietnam Base (Tools manufacturing, Hand Tools) are in export-oriented manufacturing where cost-squeeze from global supply chain competition is intense — even if statutory minimums hold, overtime enforcement and contractor conversion are likely to accelerate.

country_signal: Over 5 years, Argentina's trajectory is clearly negative for worker remuneration and moderately negative for labour rights. If Milei-era deregulation is sustained — reducing collective bargaining coverage, increasing fixed-term/temporary contract use, and weakening union dues collection mechanisms — SDG 8.8.2 scores will worsen, with a realistic pathway to entering a higher quintile. On remuneration, even if nominal wages recover, the destruction of purchasing power and reduced injury/sick pay coverage (currently 48.9%) under austerity programmes creates a deteriorating picture. The manufacturing and office/HQ workers at Pilar and LATAM HQ face this context directly.

country_signal: Over 5 years, Mexico's labour reform trajectory points toward a genuine improvement in SDG 8.8.2 scores as legacy protection contracts are replaced with democratically-elected unions. Principal risk: organised crime influence on union politics in some regions could undermine the authenticity of reform gains.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

This is the key sector where even countries with strong institutional frameworks (USA High labour rights baseline already) face practical rights deterioration as the employment relationship is increasingly mediated by technology rather than human management.

country_signal: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

country_signal: Over 5 years, Brazil's labour rights trajectory is modestly improving under Lula administration policies, but the structural constraints (informal economy ~40% of workforce, weak enforcement in rural/informal sectors) prevent a quintile shift within 5 years. Sustained Bolsa Família expansion and above-inflation minimum wage increases are improving remuneration sub-scores. GOJO Brasil in São Paulo operates in the formal economy where rights and remuneration protections are stronger than national averages suggest.
sector_signal: 5-year: Consumer-brand cost-squeeze intensification is the primary sector risk driver. As retailer power increases and private-label competition grows, branded manufacturers (SC Johnson, GOJO, Hain Celestial, JPMS) face sustained margin pressure. The supply chain response that least affects consumer price is squeezing labour costs. In countries with strong institutional frameworks (EU, Australia, UK), this pressure is mediated through collective agreements and minimum wage floors. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam, Argentina, South Africa), it translates to overtime compliance erosion, benefit reduction, and increased contractor/temp worker substitution — all rights issues even if nominal wages hold.

country_signal: Over 5 years, China's institutional labour rights framework shows no improvement pathway. The ACFTU reform agenda under Xi prioritises production continuity over worker advocacy. Automation in Chinese manufacturing is accelerating at state-directed pace — but unlike Germany's co-determination model, there is no institutional framework for workers to participate in or be compensated for automation-driven restructuring. Manufacturing workers displaced by automation have limited recourse. Government social insurance for work injuries (35.9% coverage) is likely to worsen as gig/platform economy expands without commensurate coverage. EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will add compliance pressure but will not change domestic rights.

sector_signal: 5-year: This is the highest-risk sector in the dataset for worker rights deterioration. Toy assembly, commodity fastener, and basic hand tool manufacturing are undergoing rapid automation adoption (Fanuc, Kuka robots in Chinese facilities; vision-guided assembly robots in Vietnamese plants). As automation reduces headcount, remaining workers face increased productivity targets, algorithmic monitoring, and a weakened bargaining position (easier to threaten replacement). In countries without institutional worker voice (China, Vietnam, Thailand, India), this creates a direct rights deterioration pathway that the institutional score may lag in capturing. Consumer resistance to toy/tool price increases means cost-squeeze is near-absolute — there is no slack for voluntary rights improvements.

sector_signal: 5-year: Consumer-brand cost-squeeze intensification is the primary sector risk driver. As retailer power increases and private-label competition grows, branded manufacturers (SC Johnson, GOJO, Hain Celestial, JPMS) face sustained margin pressure. The supply chain response that least affects consumer price is squeezing labour costs. In countries with strong institutional frameworks (EU, Australia, UK), this pressure is mediated through collective agreements and minimum wage floors. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam, Argentina, South Africa), it translates to overtime compliance erosion, benefit reduction, and increased contractor/temp worker substitution — all rights issues even if nominal wages hold.

country_signal: Screws, Bolts and Nuts manufacturing (GreatStar Thailand) represents the most automation-exposed and cost-squeeze-vulnerable sector in this dataset. Over 5 years: commodity fastener manufacturing faces intense price pressure from Vietnamese and Indonesian competitors; Thai minimum wage in this sector is already at near-subsistence levels; automation of this sector (robotic assembly lines for standard fasteners) threatens both employment volumes and the wage floor as contractors replace permanent workers. With no effective union voice to resist this, the remuneration risk trajectory is toward Very High. Thailand's overall labour rights institutional score is unlikely to improve in this sector context.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

sector_signal: 5-year: Distribution and logistics is undergoing the fastest automation deployment of any sector in this dataset. Full warehouse automation rollout timelines of 5-8 years mean that by 2029-30, most large DC operations will be substantially automated. Remaining workers will be in highly monitored, productivity-intensive roles with reduced rights protection (algorithmic management systems are not subject to collective bargaining in most jurisdictions). This is the key sector where even countries with strong institutional frameworks (USA High labour rights baseline already) face practical rights deterioration as the employment relationship is increasingly mediated by technology rather than human management.

Remaining workers will be in highly monitored, productivity-intensive roles with reduced rights protection (algorithmic management systems are not subject to collective bargaining in most jurisdictions). This is the key sector where even countries with strong institutional frameworks (USA High labour rights baseline already) face practical rights deterioration as the employment relationship is increasingly mediated by technology rather than human management.
context: COUNTRY SIGNAL: Over 5 years, China's institutional labour rights framework shows no improvement pathway. The ACFTU reform agenda under Xi prioritises production continuity over worker advocacy. Automation in Chinese manufacturing is accelerating at state-directed pace — but unlike Germany's co-determination model, there is no institutional framework for workers to participate in or be compensated for automation-driven restructuring. Manufacturing workers displaced by automation have limited recourse. Government social insurance for work injuries (35.9% coverage) is likely to worsen as gig/platform economy expands without commensurate coverage. EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will add compliance pressure but will not change domestic rights. monitoring_signals: ACFTU reform signals; ILO SDG 8.8.2 China score; UFLPA and EU FL Reg enforcement; labour unrest tracking (China Labour Bulletin); automation deployment rates in manufacturing

country_signal: EU Platform Work Directive and CSDDD will strengthen worker rights framework over 5 years. National minimum wage indexed to cost of living. Structural protections for professional/office workers at Perrigo Ireland and Hain Cully & Sully are strong and improving.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.

sector_signal: 5-year trajectory: technical and regulated manufacturing roles are partially automation-resistant due to regulatory compliance requirements. However, AI-assisted quality control and process optimisation will reduce headcount in some inspection and operator roles. In countries with institutional voice (EU), workers in declining roles will be retrained through works council agreements. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam), displaced workers have no institutional recourse.

SECTOR SIGNAL: 5-year: Distribution and logistics is undergoing the fastest automation deployment of any sector in this dataset. Full warehouse automation rollout timelines of 5-8 years mean that by 2029-30, most large DC operations will be substantially automated. Remaining workers will be in highly monitored, productivity-intensive roles with reduced rights protection (algorithmic management systems are not subject to collective bargaining in most jurisdictions). This is the key sector where even countries with strong institutional frameworks (USA High labour rights baseline already) face practical rights deterioration as the employment relationship is increasingly mediated by technology rather than human management.
monitoring_signals: PRO Act legislative progress; NLRB rule-making survival under judicial review; US manufacturing union density statistics; BLS contingent worker data; warehouse automation injury rate statistics

country_signal: Two scenarios over 5 years: (1) Post-conflict normalisation — migrant worker exploitation diminishes, Histadrut and labour inspection return to full capacity, institutional scores maintained at Very Low. (2) Prolonged conflict and structural dependence on migrant workers — ILO may begin capturing deterioration in SDG 8.8.2 sub-scores for non-citizen worker rights. Perrigo Yeruham operates in a pharmaceutical context where Israeli domestic workers (who have very strong protections) predominate, so direct exposure to migrant worker deterioration is limited.
sector_signal: 5-year trajectory: technical and regulated manufacturing roles are partially automation-resistant due to regulatory compliance requirements. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam), displaced workers have no institutional recourse.

country_signal: Over 5 years, Mexico's labour reform trajectory points toward a genuine improvement in SDG 8.8.2 scores as legacy protection contracts are replaced with democratically-elected unions. If the reform is maintained under the Sheinbaum administration (signs are positive — she has reaffirmed USMCA labour commitments), Mexico could shift from the High to Medium quintile for labour rights by 2028-29. Nearshoring-driven wage growth (particularly in Coahuila/Nuevo León manufacturing corridors relevant to Torreon and Toluca facilities) may also improve remuneration sub-scores. Principal risk: organised crime influence on union politics in some regions could undermine the authenticity of reform gains.

country_signal: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.

country_signal: France's institutional labour framework is durable. CSDDD transposition will add supply chain obligations. Risk: continued pension reform debates and macro-level austerity pressures may reduce real wage growth. But no quintile shift in either labour rights or remuneration expected over 5 years.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.

sector_signal: 5-year: Consumer-brand cost-squeeze intensification is the primary sector risk driver. As retailer power increases and private-label competition grows, branded manufacturers (SC Johnson, GOJO, Hain Celestial, JPMS) face sustained margin pressure. country_signal: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.
sector_signal: 5-year trajectory: technical and regulated manufacturing roles are partially automation-resistant due to regulatory compliance requirements. In countries with institutional voice (EU), workers in declining roles will be retrained through works council agreements. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam), displaced workers have no institutional recourse.

The supply chain response that least affects consumer price is squeezing labour costs. In countries with strong institutional frameworks (EU, Australia, UK), this pressure is mediated through collective agreements and minimum wage floors. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam, Argentina, South Africa), it translates to overtime compliance erosion, benefit reduction, and increased contractor/temp worker substitution — all rights issues even if nominal wages hold.
context: COUNTRY SIGNAL: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term. country_signal: CSDDD transposition will further strengthen supply chain labour obligations. Germany's institutional worker rights framework is among the most durable globally. The key 5-year watch: automation in manufacturing (particularly relevant to DreBo Werkzeugfabrik / Interchangeable Tools) is being managed through co-determination processes, meaning automation displacement does not translate to rights erosion in the German model — works councils actively participate in restructuring decisions. This is the critical distinction from US/Asian manufacturing contexts.

country_signal: Over 5 years, Hong Kong's labour rights trajectory is one of the clearest deterioration cases in the dataset. As Hong Kong's legal framework converges with PRC norms, independent worker advocacy becomes increasingly impossible. The next ILO SDG 8.8.2 data cycle (2026-27) is expected to begin capturing this deterioration — moving Hong Kong's score significantly upward from the current understated baseline. A move to the High quintile is the realistic 5-year outcome. ZURU Global HQ operates as a management and coordination function for professional workers who have greater individual market leverage, but the institutional framework is weakening.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.

country_signal: Over 5 years, China's institutional labour rights framework shows no improvement pathway. The ACFTU reform agenda under Xi prioritises production continuity over worker advocacy. Automation in Chinese manufacturing is accelerating at state-directed pace — but unlike Germany's co-determination model, there is no institutional framework for workers to participate in or be compensated for automation-driven restructuring. Manufacturing workers displaced by automation have limited recourse. Government social insurance for work injuries (35.9% coverage) is likely to worsen as gig/platform economy expands without commensurate coverage. EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will add compliance pressure but will not change domestic rights.

country_signal: Labour Codes 2020 full implementation across all states (target 2025-27) will worsen institutional sub-scores as fixed-term employment proliferates and union formation thresholds rise. The IT/software sector (ZURU Ahmedabad, ZURU Kolkata) has historically been exempt from many labour protections under the Shops and Establishments Acts, a gap that the Labour Codes widen rather than close. Minimum wage ($233/month PPP) is likely to rise with economic growth but will not exit the Very High remuneration risk quintile within 5 years. Tech sector AI-driven productivity gains are reducing headcount in Indian software outsourcing — workers losing jobs have limited institutional recourse.

country_signal: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust.

country_signal: Over 5 years, China's institutional labour rights framework shows no improvement pathway. The ACFTU reform agenda under Xi prioritises production continuity over worker advocacy. Automation in Chinese manufacturing is accelerating at state-directed pace — but unlike Germany's co-determination model, there is no institutional framework for workers to participate in or be compensated for automation-driven restructuring. Manufacturing workers displaced by automation have limited recourse. Government social insurance for work injuries (35.9% coverage) is likely to worsen as gig/platform economy expands without commensurate coverage. EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will add compliance pressure but will not change domestic rights.

sector_signal: 5-year: Distribution and logistics is undergoing the fastest automation deployment of any sector in this dataset. Full warehouse automation rollout timelines of 5-8 years mean that by 2029-30, most large DC operations will be substantially automated. Remaining workers will be in highly monitored, productivity-intensive roles with reduced rights protection (algorithmic management systems are not subject to collective bargaining in most jurisdictions). This is the key sector where even countries with strong institutional frameworks (USA High labour rights baseline already) face practical rights deterioration as the employment relationship is increasingly mediated by technology rather than human management.

Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.

sector_signal: 5-year: Consumer-brand cost-squeeze intensification is the primary sector risk driver. As retailer power increases and private-label competition grows, branded manufacturers (SC Johnson, GOJO, Hain Celestial, JPMS) face sustained margin pressure. The supply chain response that least affects consumer price is squeezing labour costs. In countries with strong institutional frameworks (EU, Australia, UK), this pressure is mediated through collective agreements and minimum wage floors. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam, Argentina, South Africa), it translates to overtime compliance erosion, benefit reduction, and increased contractor/temp worker substitution — all rights issues even if nominal wages hold.

In countries with institutional voice (EU), workers in declining roles will be retrained through works council agreements. In high-risk countries (China, Vietnam), displaced workers have no institutional recourse.
context: COUNTRY SIGNAL: Over 5 years, US labour rights trajectory is highly variable. Two divergent scenarios: (1) Continued Republican legislative dominance — PRO Act remains stalled, NLRB rule-making reversed, RTW provisions expanded; SDG 8.8.2 score may worsen. (2) Political shift enabling PRO Act-equivalent legislation — would materially improve right to association sub-scores; score could improve to Medium risk. Neither scenario produces a cluster migration within 5 years; the relevant question is whether the High rating is maintained or worsens. Remuneration risk stays Low: minimum wage indexed in most states; social protection for work-related injury (87.2% coverage) is structurally robust. country_signal: If the Employment Rights Bill is fully implemented (likely by 2026-27), the UK's SDG 8.8.2 score will improve, potentially shifting the labour rights quintile from Low toward Very Low over the 5-year horizon. The new right to sectoral fair pay agreements and strengthened union recognition procedures directly improve the sub-scores the ILO measures. Remuneration risk will remain Very Low with continued real-wage growth above inflation commitment. Principal risk: a future Conservative government reversing the Bill — but this is a 5-year tail risk, not near-term.

country_signal: Netherlands is addressing platform/flex worker rights through EU Directive on platform work (2024), which will improve collective bargaining access for gig workers over 5 years. This may improve ILO sub-scores. CSDDD will strengthen supply chain obligations. Structural remuneration protections remain robust.
sector_signal: 5-year AI automation risk for office roles is real but concentrated in routine cognitive tasks (data entry, report generation, basic analysis). Senior, strategic and creative professional roles retain strong market leverage. Countries with works councils (Germany, Netherlands, Austria) will manage AI deployment through co-determination — workers in these jurisdictions have institutional recourse. Workers in countries without institutional voice (China, Vietnam, India) face AI-driven surveillance and algorithmic management with no rights-based pushback mechanism.
